questions.stem: In a SQL interview, explain INNER JOIN versus LEFT JOIN on matching keys. Which answer is most accurate?
options: INNER keeps only matching rows; LEFT keeps all left rows, NULLs on right miss
LEFT JOIN drops unmatched left rows; INNER keeps every left row always
They are identical except LEFT JOIN forbids ON predicates
INNER JOIN can only join two tables; LEFT JOIN needs three
questions.explanation: INNER requires a match; LEFT preserves every left-side row and pads non-matches with NULL.

questions.stem: What does a CROSS JOIN produce between tables A and B when no filter is applied?
options: Only rows where primary keys are equal
The Cartesian product of all A rows with all B rows
A full outer join of A and B automatically
A sorted merge of A and B on the first column
questions.explanation: CROSS JOIN is every combination of rows — |A| × |B| unless filtered later.

questions.stem: WHERE versus HAVING — when must you filter with HAVING instead of WHERE?
options: When joining tables; HAVING is required for every JOIN
When filtering raw rows before any aggregation ever runs
When filtering aggregated groups after GROUP BY has computed results
When comparing NULLs; WHERE cannot use IS NULL
questions.explanation: WHERE filters rows pre-aggregation; HAVING filters groups after aggregate computation.

questions.stem: GROUP BY interview staple: which columns may appear in SELECT with an aggregate?
options: Any column from the table without restriction
Only columns that are indexed uniquely
Only primary key columns regardless of GROUP BY
Aggregates plus columns listed in GROUP BY (engine rules apply)
questions.explanation: Non-aggregated SELECT expressions must be functionally dependent on the GROUP BY keys.

questions.stem: Three-valued logic: what is the result of comparing NULL = NULL in a WHERE clause?
options: UNKNOWN, so the row is filtered out by WHERE
TRUE, so the row always passes the filter
FALSE, but OR with TRUE still fails always
A runtime error that aborts the entire query
questions.explanation: NULL comparisons yield UNKNOWN; WHERE keeps only rows where the predicate is TRUE.

questions.stem: How should you correctly test for a NULL column value in a SQL WHERE clause?
options: WHERE column = NULL using ordinary equality
WHERE column IS NULL (or IS NOT NULL)
WHERE column == None as in Python syntax
WHERE ISNULL(column = TRUE) only
questions.explanation: Use IS NULL / IS NOT NULL; equality with NULL never yields TRUE.

questions.stem: EXISTS versus IN for a subquery checking related rows — common interview guidance?
options: EXISTS cannot reference outer columns; IN always can
IN always outperforms EXISTS on every engine forever
EXISTS short-circuits on first match; IN materializes a value list (optimizer may rewrite)
They are illegal in the same SQL standard version
questions.explanation: EXISTS is a semi-join style existence check; IN compares against a set (watch NULLs carefully).

questions.stem: What does BEGIN (or START TRANSACTION) followed by COMMIT accomplish in SQL?
options: Disables indexes until ROLLBACK is issued
Only locks the schema catalog without applying DML
Runs each statement in its own auto-commit after the first
Marks a durable all-or-nothing unit of work across the enclosed statements
questions.explanation: Explicit transactions group statements so COMMIT persists all or ROLLBACK undoes all.

questions.stem: DELETE versus TRUNCATE versus DROP — which distinction is interview-correct?
options: DELETE removes rows (logged); TRUNCATE empties a table fast; DROP removes the object
TRUNCATE drops the table definition; DROP only clears rows
DELETE and DROP are synonyms; TRUNCATE is for indexes only
DROP preserves constraints; TRUNCATE always fires every row trigger identically
questions.explanation: DELETE is row DML; TRUNCATE is bulk empty (DDL-ish); DROP removes the table itself.

questions.stem: What is a correlated subquery in SQL, and why can it become expensive?
options: A subquery that never references outer columns and runs once
A subquery depending on outer-row values, potentially re-evaluated per outer row
A JOIN written with USING that the planner always forbids
A window function that cannot use PARTITION BY
questions.explanation: Correlation ties inner query to outer row context; naive plans may run it repeatedly.

questions.stem: FULL OUTER JOIN semantics: which rows appear in the result set of the join?
options: Only matches; unmatched sides are discarded always
Cartesian product with no NULL padding ever
Matches plus unmatched left rows and unmatched right rows with NULLs
Only unmatched rows; matches are excluded
questions.explanation: FULL OUTER keeps matches and preserves unmatched rows from both sides.

questions.stem: SELECT DISTINCT versus GROUP BY for unique values — when are they similar?
options: Only inside UPDATE statements, never SELECT
Never; DISTINCT cannot remove duplicates of any kind
Only when using HAVING with no GROUP BY clause
When grouping by the same columns you want unique without aggregates
questions.explanation: DISTINCT and GROUP BY-on-those-columns both collapse duplicate combinations.

questions.stem: Why might an index on (status) fail to help WHERE LOWER(status) = 'open'?
options: A function on the column can prevent a plain index seek unless an expression index exists
Because indexes never help equality predicates
Because LOWER always forces a full outer join
Because status columns cannot be indexed in SQL
questions.explanation: Wrapping the column hides the indexed value; use a functional/expression index or rewrite.

questions.stem: COUNT(*) versus COUNT(column) — what is the key difference when NULLs appear?
options: COUNT(*) ignores all rows; COUNT(column) counts every row
COUNT(*) counts rows; COUNT(column) skips NULLs in that column
They are identical including NULL handling always
COUNT(column) counts distinct values only by default
questions.explanation: COUNT(*) counts rows; COUNT(expr) counts non-NULL evaluations of expr.

questions.stem: What does ORDER BY do relative to LIMIT/OFFSET pagination patterns in practice?
options: ORDER BY is optional and LIMIT always returns a deterministic page
LIMIT sorts rows automatically without ORDER BY
Stable pagination needs ORDER BY (ideally unique) before LIMIT/OFFSET
OFFSET cannot be used with ORDER BY in standard SQL
questions.explanation: Without ORDER BY, LIMIT pages are undefined; ties need a unique sort key.

questions.stem: UNION versus UNION ALL — which distinction should you give in a SQL interview?
options: UNION requires ORDER BY; UNION ALL forbids it
UNION ALL removes duplicates; UNION keeps them
They differ only in column alias rules, not row sets
UNION removes duplicate rows; UNION ALL keeps duplicates and is usually cheaper
questions.explanation: UNION deduplicates (extra work); UNION ALL concatenates as-is.

questions.stem: Primary key constraint in SQL: what does it enforce on the designated column set?
options: Uniqueness and NOT NULL on the key columns
Only uniqueness; NULLs are always allowed in PK columns
Only NOT NULL; duplicates are fine
That the column must be a foreign key elsewhere
questions.explanation: PRIMARY KEY implies UNIQUE + NOT NULL for the key attributes.

questions.stem: EXPLAIN (or EXPLAIN ANALYZE) — what interview insight should you cite?
options: It rewrites application code into stored procedures automatically
It shows the planner's access path: scans, joins, estimated/actual costs
It always forces the use of every available index
It only works for INSERT and never for SELECT
questions.explanation: EXPLAIN reveals how the engine plans (and with ANALYZE, how it executed) the query.

questions.stem: Self-join use case: employees table with manager_id referencing employee id. Best description?
options: A CROSS JOIN that forbids ON conditions
A join that is only legal on temporary tables
Joining a table to itself to relate rows within the same relation
A join that always produces a Cartesian product only
questions.explanation: Self-joins pair rows of one table (e.g. employee to manager) via aliases.

questions.stem: ROLLBACK after BEGIN: what happens to uncommitted DML in that transaction?
options: Changes are persisted because DDL already flushed them
Other sessions' committed work is also reverted
Only the last statement is undone; earlier ones stay
All uncommitted changes in the transaction are undone
questions.explanation: ROLLBACK aborts the current transaction and discards its uncommitted effects.

questions.stem: Window function high-level: how does ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY dept ORDER BY salary) differ from GROUP BY?
options: Windows compute over partitions while keeping detail rows; GROUP BY collapses groups
Window functions collapse rows; GROUP BY keeps every detail row
They are identical; ROW_NUMBER is just syntax sugar for HAVING
Window functions cannot use ORDER BY inside OVER
questions.explanation: Windows add analytic columns without collapsing; GROUP BY reduces to one row per group.

questions.stem: RANK versus DENSE_RANK versus ROW_NUMBER for tied salaries — key distinction?
options: DENSE_RANK assigns random ranks; RANK is sequential always
ROW_NUMBER unique per partition; RANK gaps after ties; DENSE_RANK no gaps
ROW_NUMBER leaves gaps; RANK never ties by definition
All three always produce identical integers for every input
questions.explanation: ROW_NUMBER is unique; RANK skips after ties; DENSE_RANK continues without gaps.

questions.stem: Anti-join pattern: find customers with no orders. Which approach is idiomatic?
options: INNER JOIN orders and filter WHERE order_id IS NOT NULL
CROSS JOIN orders without a WHERE clause
LEFT JOIN orders ... WHERE orders.id IS NULL (or NOT EXISTS)
FULL OUTER JOIN and keep only matched pairs
questions.explanation: Anti-join: preserve left rows whose right side is NULL, or use NOT EXISTS.

questions.stem: IN (subquery) when the subquery can return NULL — what subtle pitfall appears?
options: Engines ignore NULLs in IN lists with no semantic effect
IN always errors if any NULL appears in the subquery
NULL in the list makes every IN predicate TRUE
NOT IN becomes UNKNOWN for all rows if the subquery set contains NULL
questions.explanation: NOT IN with NULL in the set yields UNKNOWN, filtering out all candidates unexpectedly.

questions.stem: Index nested-loop join versus hash join: what intuition should EXPLAIN give you?
options: Hash join builds a hash of one input then probes; nested loop probes per outer row
Hash join always requires an index on both sides
Nested loops never use indexes; hash joins always do
They produce different result sets for the same INNER JOIN
questions.explanation: Different physical join algorithms; same logical INNER JOIN, different cost profiles.

questions.stem: Sargable predicate: which form is more likely to use a B-tree index on created_at?
options: WHERE YEAR(created_at) = 2024 wrapping the column
WHERE created_at >= '2024-01-01' AND created_at < '2025-01-01'
WHERE CAST(created_at AS text) LIKE '2024%'
WHERE created_at::date + 0 = CURRENT_DATE
questions.explanation: Keep the indexed column bare; range bounds remain seekable on a B-tree.

questions.stem: SELECT FOR UPDATE (pessimistic locking) — what does it typically do to rows?
options: Converts the SELECT into a DELETE automatically
Creates a new snapshot that ignores all future commits forever
Locks selected rows so concurrent writers wait until this transaction ends
Disables MVCC for the whole database cluster
questions.explanation: FOR UPDATE takes row locks for update intent until COMMIT/ROLLBACK.

questions.stem: CTE (WITH clause) readability versus performance — balanced interview take?
options: CTEs always materialize and are always slower than subqueries
CTEs replace the need for indexes on joined columns
CTEs are illegal inside transactions
CTEs clarify structure; engines may inline or materialize depending on version/hints
questions.explanation: WITH aids clarity; optimization behavior (inline vs materialize) is engine-specific.

questions.stem: Aggregate FILTER / CASE pattern: count only active users in one pass. Best idea?
options: COUNT(*) FILTER (WHERE active) or SUM(CASE WHEN active THEN 1 ELSE 0 END)
Always run separate queries per status with UNION ALL only
Use CROSS JOIN against a status dimension mandatorily
HAVING active = true without GROUP BY
questions.explanation: Conditional aggregates compute multiple measures in a single grouped scan.

questions.stem: Upsert (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT / MERGE) — what concurrency problem does it solve?
options: Deleting all duplicates before every insert manually
Insert-or-update atomically when a unique key already exists
Replacing TRUNCATE for emptying tables faster
Forcing SERIALIZABLE isolation on every session
questions.explanation: Upsert handles race-prone insert-or-update behind a unique constraint safely.

questions.stem: Covering index for SELECT id, email WHERE status = 'active' — what columns help?
options: A hash index on a different table's primary key
Only an index on email with no status column
An index on (status) including or composed with id and email
No index; covering requires a materialized view always
questions.explanation: Filter key plus selected columns in the index enables index-only retrieval.

questions.stem: OFFSET large N for deep pages — why do seniors push keyset pagination?
options: OFFSET is O(1) regardless of N on all engines
LIMIT without OFFSET is illegal in SQL
Keyset pagination cannot use indexes by definition
Large OFFSET still scans/skips many rows; seek by last-seen key is cheaper
questions.explanation: Deep OFFSET is expensive; WHERE (sort_key) > :last uses an index seek.

questions.stem: Prepared statements / bind parameters — main benefits beyond convenience?
options: Plan reuse and safer parameterization against SQL injection
They disable the query planner for security reasons
They force full table scans to avoid index hijacking
They convert all DML into DDL automatically
questions.explanation: Parameters separate code from data and often allow cached plans.

questions.stem: NULL-safe equality in joins: why can ordinary = miss matches you might expect?
options: Because joins ignore ON and only use WHERE
Because NULL = NULL is UNKNOWN, so NULL keys do not match
Because INNER JOIN forbids equality predicates
Because NULLs are always coerced to zero in joins
questions.explanation: Standard = does not treat NULLs as equal; use IS NOT DISTINCT FROM where supported.

questions.stem: VACUUM / dead tuple cleanup (MVCC engines) — why does UPDATE leave cleanup work?
options: Dead tuples only appear after DROP TABLE
UPDATE always rewrites SQL text only, never storage
Updates create new row versions; old versions need reclaiming later
COMMIT deletes indexes that must be rebuilt each time
questions.explanation: MVCC UPDATE/DELETE leave obsolete versions; vacuum/purge reclaims space.

questions.stem: Partial / filtered index WHERE status = 'open' — when is that pattern valuable?
options: Only for FULL OUTER JOIN performance
When every query needs all statuses equally
When you want to index columns that do not exist
When most queries filter that subset and the predicate is common
questions.explanation: Smaller indexes targeting hot predicates reduce size and improve cache locality.

questions.stem: SAVEPOINT inside a transaction — what can you do that plain COMMIT cannot?
options: Roll back to a named point while keeping earlier work in the same transaction
Make the entire database durable without WAL
Escape SERIALIZABLE into READ UNCOMMITTED mid-statement
Share locks across unrelated database clusters
questions.explanation: SAVEPOINTs allow partial rollback without aborting the whole transaction.

questions.stem: Lateral join / correlated FROM subquery — what capability does it add?
options: Forbidding correlation so every subquery runs once globally
Referencing left-hand columns when computing a right-hand row set per input
Replacing GROUP BY with DISTINCT always
Disabling the use of indexes on joined keys
questions.explanation: LATERAL lets the right side depend on the current left-side row.

questions.stem: Cardinality estimate errors from stale stats — symptom you should recognize?
options: PRIMARY KEY constraints stop enforcing uniqueness
COMMIT starts failing with disk-full for every SELECT
EXPLAIN chooses a terrible join order or nested loop on huge sets
NULL comparisons begin returning TRUE for all rows
questions.explanation: Bad estimates mislead the planner into suboptimal join/scan strategies.

questions.stem: Idempotent DELETE of stale sessions: DELETE ... WHERE id = $1 RETURNING *. Why RETURNING?
options: To bypass foreign key checks on child tables
Because DELETE cannot use WHERE without RETURNING
To convert DELETE into TRUNCATE automatically
To learn whether a row was actually removed in this attempt
questions.explanation: RETURNING reports affected rows so callers distinguish no-op retries from real deletes.

questions.stem: Skip-scan / Index Skip Scan idea when a composite index is (tenant_id, created_at) but filter is only on created_at — senior framing?
options: Optimizer may probe distinct tenant_id values then range-scan; not always available
The trailing column is always seekable without the leading column on every engine
Composite indexes ignore column order completely
Skip scan only applies to hash indexes
questions.explanation: Some engines can skip across leading-column distincts; otherwise rewrite or re-index.

questions.stem: Serializable anomaly under concurrent INSERT of unique emails — classic failure mode?
options: UNIQUE constraints are ignored under SERIALIZABLE
Two transactions both see absence, both insert, one hits unique violation or one aborts
Phantoms cannot occur for uniqueness checks
BEGIN disables unique indexes until COMMIT
questions.explanation: Race on 'not exists then insert' needs unique constraints and proper isolation/retry.

questions.stem: Bitmap heap scan after an index: when does EXPLAIN often show this pattern?
options: Only for CROSS JOIN without WHERE
Only when no indexes exist on the table at all
Many matching TIDs are collected from indexes then sorted/visited on the heap
When the query is a DDL ALTER TABLE
questions.explanation: Bitmap scans combine index hits then visit heap pages more sequentially.

questions.stem: Outbox pattern with SQL transactions: why insert business row + outbox event together?
options: Because triggers cannot run inside transactions
So the message broker can lock the database schema
To avoid using primary keys on either table
So COMMIT atomically records state change and a reliably publishable event
questions.explanation: Transactional outbox keeps DB write and eventual publish consistent without dual-write races.

questions.stem: Advisory locks for application-level critical sections — senior caveat?
options: They are session/transaction scoped tokens; misuse causes deadlocks or forgotten holds
They replace the need for transactions entirely
They automatically cover every row touched by SELECT
They make EXPLAIN show covering indexes always
questions.explanation: Advisory locks coordinate apps but need clear ownership, timeouts, and deadlock awareness.

questions.stem: Partition pruning: how should a large fact table's WHERE clause be written?
options: Always wrap the partition key in a function to help pruning
Predicate on the partition key so the planner can skip irrelevant partitions
Avoid mentioning the partition key so all partitions scan faster
Use CROSS JOIN across partitions to force parallelism
questions.explanation: Sargable predicates on the partition key enable partition elimination.

questions.stem: Write skew example in SQL: two transactions enforce 'at least one admin' via SELECT then UPDATE. Fix?
options: Replace UPDATE with TRUNCATE on the users table
Switch both sessions to READ UNCOMMITTED
Use SERIALIZABLE/SSI or lock the constraint rows/predicate properly
Remove the UNIQUE constraint on admin emails
questions.explanation: Predicate integrity needs serializable control or explicit locking of the checked set.

questions.stem: Histogram / MCV stats and skewed joins — why do they matter at senior level?
options: They only apply to temporary tables
They replace indexes for all equality lookups
They force nested-loop joins exclusively
They help the planner estimate selectivity when values are unevenly distributed
questions.explanation: Skewed data fools uniform estimates; richer stats improve join/filter choices.

questions.stem: Online DDL concurrent with DML: what risk should you name even with non-blocking tools?
options: Replication lag, lock queues on cutover, or inconsistent reads during backfill windows
That COMMIT becomes optional during the migration
That primary keys are dropped permanently after success
That EXPLAIN stops working until restart
questions.explanation: Even careful online schema changes have operational windows and replication effects.

questions.stem: Recursive CTE for org chart depth — termination concern you must address?
options: Recursive CTEs cannot join tables by definition
Cycles in the graph can recurse forever without a visited/path guard
WITH RECURSIVE forbids UNION ALL in standard SQL
Depth is always limited to three levels by the engine
questions.explanation: Detect cycles (path arrays/sets) or depth caps so hierarchy walks terminate.
